claim: 52% of US Gen Z ages 18–25 report anxiety as a significant mental health concern, yet only 37% of those sought professional help.
source_citation: American Psychological Association, Stress in America 2023, n=3,185
invisible_script: I know something is wrong but institutions are not trustworthy enough to disclose to.
contradiction: Gen Z identifies as the most mental-health-aware generation yet engages with formal services at lower rates than prior generations.

claim: 47% of Japanese ages 18–29 cited global conflict as a top anxiety source; hikikomori prevalence estimated at 1.46 million.
evidence: Cabinet Office survey 2023; Cabinet Office hikikomori estimate 2023
invisible_script: Safety exists only in withdrawal — the outside world is unpredictable and threatening.
contradiction: Japan's social cohesion metrics remain high while individual youth withdrawal rates reach historic levels — collective norms coexist with private retreat.

claim: 59% of US Gen Z report being disengaged at work (Gallup 2023), while 67% list financial security as their top life goal.
evidence: Gallup State of the Global Workplace 2023; Deloitte Global Gen Z Survey 2023, n=22,841
invisible_script: The system rewards compliance and punishes ambition — reduce exposure.
contradiction: Gen Z simultaneously pursues financial security as top goal and disengage from the primary mechanism (work) by which they could achieve it.

claim: Japan youth unemployment 4.3% (2023) but non-regular employment among under-35s at 31% — employment without security or career trajectory.
evidence: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare Japan 2023
invisible_script: Being employed is not the same as having a future.
contradiction: Official employment figures appear healthy while structural precarity (non-regular, part-time, contract) defines the experience of nearly one-third of young workers.

claim: China's tangping (lying flat) movement, originating 2021, explicitly rejects 996 work culture and competitive consumption, representing a cohort-level refusal of the prevailing success script.
evidence: Social media analysis; Pew Research China 2022
invisible_script: Participation in the system costs more than it returns — the rational move is withdrawal.
contradiction: The same cohort that rejects 996 work culture shows high luxury goods consumption — selective participation in consumption while rejecting productive participation.

claim: 75% of Gen Z globally rate climate change as their top concern (Deloitte 2023), yet Gen Z shows highest fast-fashion consumption of any living generation.
evidence: Deloitte Global Gen Z Survey 2023, n=22,841; ThredUp 2023 Resale Report
invisible_script: Individual action is insufficient — but collective action feels inaccessible so I maintain my lifestyle.
contradiction: Gen Z's stated climate concern is the highest ever recorded, while their actual consumption behavior (fast fashion, air travel, streaming carbon) contradicts stated values.

claim: 73% of Japanese ages 15–29 report climate worry, yet only 12% took any action beyond recycling in the prior year (Ministry of Environment, 2022).
evidence: Ministry of Environment Japan, Environmental Awareness Survey 2022
invisible_script: Concern is sufficient — action requires institutional infrastructure I don't have access to.
contradiction: Japan's Gen Z shows highest survey-reported climate concern in East Asia alongside lowest per-capita individual climate action rate in the same cohort.

claim: Gen Z in conflict-adjacent countries shows compassion fatigue: high initial engagement with conflict news followed by withdrawal and emotional numbing.
source_citation: Reuters Institute for the Study of Journalism, Digital News Report 2023
invisible_script: Sustained attention to others' suffering is not survivable — I must disengage to function.
contradiction: Gen Z identifies as most globally aware generation yet social media algorithm exposure to conflict is geographically and ideologically siloed — different cohorts watch entirely different versions of the same conflict.

claim: 83% of Japanese oppose constitutional revision to allow collective defense (Asahi Shimbun 2022), yet Japan's defense budget reached ¥6.8 trillion in FY2023 — highest since WWII.
evidence: Asahi Shimbun Constitutional Survey 2022; Japan Ministry of Defense FY2023 budget
invisible_script: I hold pacifist values that the political system is overriding — my opinion does not translate to policy.
contradiction: Supermajority pacifist sentiment among youth coexists with historically high defense spending — political outcomes diverge structurally from generational preferences.

claim: Gen Z is most credentialled generation in history (57% enrolled/completed college, NCES 2022) yet 52% of recent US graduates employed in roles not requiring their degree.
evidence: NCES 2022; Federal Reserve Bank of New York, The Labor Market for Recent College Graduates, 2023
invisible_script: The credential was the path; the path has not arrived at the destination.
contradiction: Unprecedented credential attainment coexists with unprecedented credential-employment mismatch — the highest-educated generation is experiencing the lowest credential ROI.

claim: Juken (exam prep) industry revenue ¥945B in 2022; student average daily study hours outside school: 4.2 hours (MEXT 2022). 35% of Japanese ages 22–29 unsure their education prepared them for work.
evidence: MEXT Japan Education Survey 2022; Recruit Works Institute 2023
invisible_script: Effort within the system is the only path — but the system's endpoint is unclear.
contradiction: The most academically demanding youth culture in the developed world produces the highest rates of 'unsure my education was useful' — maximum input, uncertain output.

claim: Gen Z is on track to be less wealthy than their parents at equivalent age; median Gen Z wealth at 25 is 30% lower in real terms than Boomers at same age.
evidence: Federal Reserve Board, Distribution of Household Wealth, 2023
invisible_script: The social contract my parents inherited was broken before I could use it.
contradiction: 73% of Gen Z say reducing inequality is important (Pew 2022) while luxury goods consumption among Gen Z increased 26% 2020–2023 (Bain & Co) — values and behavior diverge.

claim: Japan's child poverty rate 13.5% (2021, MHLW) — highest sustained level since 1985; youth homelessness increasing in major cities despite official data undercounting.
evidence: Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare Japan, 2021
invisible_script: Poverty is invisible in Japan — to be poor is to have failed at social concealment.
contradiction: Japan's visible social prosperity (clean cities, low crime, social trust) coexists with rising child poverty that is structurally undercounted and culturally hidden.

claim: Only 34% of Gen Z trust international organisations (Edelman Trust Barometer 2023, ages 18–26), yet the same cohort shows highest rates of civic participation through informal channels.
evidence: Edelman Trust Barometer 2023; youth-led organisations raised $2.3B in civic tech/advocacy funding 2021–2023 (Candid Foundation)
invisible_script: Formal institutions failed — informal networks are doing the work.
contradiction: Record low institutional trust coexists with record high informal civic activity — Gen Z is highly civically engaged, but outside the institutions that claim to represent them.

claim: For Gen Alpha (ages 10–15), unlike Gen Z who discovered social media as adolescents, Gen Alpha was born into it — their mental health baseline has never included a pre-platform reference point.
evidence: Persona switch analysis from generational research framework
invisible_script: This is just how reality is — there is no 'before' to reference.
contradiction: Gen Alpha expresses concern about screen time while having no experiential baseline for non-screen life — concern without comparison.
